humanities_role_explanation: MMLU includes specific academic humanities subjects (e.g., history, philosophy, world religions) as target evaluation domains to measure domain expertise, while using their textual questions to assess general multi-task language understanding.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: The benchmark is entirely text-based, consisting of multiple-choice questions with four options evaluated using zero-shot and few-shot text prompts.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Evaluates declarative knowledge retrieval in history and religion, logical deduction in formal logic, moral reasoning in everyday scenarios, and legal rule application in professional law cases.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Evaluates broad multitask language understanding across 57 diverse subjects spanning humanities, social sciences, STEM, and professional domains.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates GPT-3 (up to 175B parameters), GPT-2, and UnifiedQA.

llm_is_primary_system.note: Generative LLMs and text-to-text models are the primary focus of the evaluation.

arts_humanities_data.note: Includes subjects such as History (US, European, World, and Prehistory), Philosophy (Formal Logic, Fallacies, and Ethics), and Religious Studies (World Religions).

quantitative_metrics.note: Evaluated using multi-class classification accuracy and Root Mean Squared (RMS) calibration error.

published_after_march_2023.note: Published at ICLR 2021, with preprints emerging in late 2020.

english_language.note: The paper and the benchmark dataset are written entirely in English.

summary: This paper introduces the Massive Multitask Language Understanding (MMLU) benchmark, a test spanning 57 subjects designed to measure LLM multitask knowledge and reasoning. By evaluating models in zero-shot and few-shot transfer settings across 15,908 multiple-choice questions, it aims to test the breadth and depth of real-world knowledge acquired during pretraining.
key_findings: The 175B parameter GPT-3 model achieved 43.9% average accuracy, scoring well above the 25% random baseline but far below the 89.8% human expert-level estimate. The evaluation revealed that LLM capabilities were highly lopsided, showing particular weakness in calculation-heavy STEM tasks and socially sensitive subjects like morality and law, and exhibited poor calibration with confidence scores diverging from actual accuracy by up to 24%.
review_notes: Ciop and Cip largely agreed. Ciop classified humanities_role as 'content', while Cip classified it as 'both'. I resolved this to 'both' because MMLU aims to measure domain-specific competence in humanities subjects alongside using them as content for general multitask understanding. I combined their analytical tasks to encompass factual, logical, moral, and legal reasoning.
